title: How Many Lives Does God Take in the Bible: An Investigation into a Surprisingly High Body Count
description:
in Religion |   	March 31st, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Whether or not we believe in any god, most of us here in the twenôÙty-first cenôÙtuôÙry have the
body:
in Religion |   	March 31st, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

Whether or not we believe in any god, most of us here in the twenôÙty-first cenôÙtuôÙry have the impresôÙsion of divine rulers overôÙlookôÙing humanôÙiôÙty with at least theôÙoôÙretôÙiôÙcal love and benevôÙoôÙlence. They forôÙgive us, they have plans for us, they nevôÙer close a door withôÙout openôÙing a winôÙdow, and so on. But in the parôÙticôÙuôÙlar case of the ChrisôÙtôÙian God, weãve all heard that he both giveth and taketh away, even if weãve nevôÙer so much as opened the Bible, Old TesôÙtaôÙment or New. That line comes from the Book of Job, which belongs to the Old, a text whose depicôÙtion of God may surôÙprise first-time readôÙers ã espeôÙcialôÙly in his willôÙingôÙness to cause death, the subôÙject of the HochelaôÙga video above on ãGodãs BibôÙliôÙcal Kill Count.ã
It turns out that, if you go through the King James VerôÙsion and talôÙly up every sinôÙgle perôÙson God kills on a spreadôÙsheet (a task to which HochelaôÙga creôÙator TomôÙmie Trelawny is sureôÙly among the best-suitôÙed YouTuôÙbers), you end up with a high numôÙber at the botôÙtom indeed. ãThroughôÙout the Old TesôÙtaôÙment, God is responôÙsiôÙble for a whole slew of natôÙurôÙal disôÙasôÙters,ã he says, ãfrom erasôÙing life on Earth in a world-endôÙing flood to unleashôÙing devôÙasôÙtatôÙing plagues ofã ã yes ã ãBibôÙliôÙcal proôÙporôÙtions.ã
ConôÙcerned as it is with layôÙing out Godãs law, the Old TesôÙtaôÙment, or Hebrew Bible, spends a great deal of time explainôÙing what hapôÙpens to the vioôÙlaôÙtors of those laws. In one pasôÙsage, 50,070 men are ãpulôÙverôÙized for glimpsôÙing inside the Ark of the Covenant,ã and, in anothôÙer, God sends an angel to ãwipe out 185,000 solôÙdiers in one night,ã to name just two inciôÙdents.
Trelawnyãs iniôÙtial count of the deaths the Bible attribôÙutôÙes to God comes to a preôÙcise-soundôÙing 2,559,449. But that figôÙure only includes instances in which the text specôÙiôÙfies how many peoôÙple died. SomeôÙtimes it doesôÙnãt, which requires the conôÙsciôÙenôÙtious bibôÙliôÙcal body-counter to rely on the best hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcal estiôÙmates of, for examôÙple, how many peoôÙple an army or a city ã entiôÙties the Old TesôÙtaôÙment God could anniôÙhiôÙlate with a flick of the wrist ã comôÙprised at the time, to say nothôÙing of the EarthôÙãs total popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion at the preôÙsumpôÙtive time of the Flood. Trelawny goes with 20 milôÙlion, bringôÙing the final count to 24,681,116, about the same as the entire popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion of ShangôÙhai. It may seem ironôÙic to draw a comôÙparôÙiôÙson with a city outôÙside what we could call the Judeo-ChrisôÙtôÙian world, but ChiôÙnese civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion has strict deities of its own. Run afoul of Leigong, for instance, and you could find yourôÙself struck down by a bolt of lightôÙning. But heãd sureôÙly have to get busy throwôÙing a whole lot more of them before even hopôÙing to approach the Lordãs record.

Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

body:
From Yale proôÙfesôÙsor Paul North comes a chapôÙter-by-chapôÙter study of Karl Marxãs CapôÙiôÙtal: CriôÙtique of PolitôÙiôÙcal EconôÙoôÙmy, VolôÙume 1. AccordôÙing to the descripôÙtion that accomôÙpaôÙnies the course on YouTube, this ãbook from 1872 is still the best guide to the predaôÙtoôÙry ecoôÙnomôÙic and social sysôÙtem withôÙin which we live. The book solves five basic mysôÙterôÙies in our social world. The mysôÙterôÙies are: why social classôÙes strugôÙgle against one anothôÙer, why human beings are in the thrall of things, how a quanôÙtiôÙty of monôÙey turns into more monôÙey withôÙout seemôÙing to add anyôÙthing, why some peoôÙple are forced to work and the more they work the less they make proôÙporôÙtionôÙal to their effort, and finalôÙly, and why it is so hard to transôÙform the sysôÙtem for the betôÙter.ã You can watch the 19 lecôÙtures from the course in the playlist above.
Prof. North is the co-ediôÙtor of the new EngôÙlish transôÙlaôÙtion and critôÙiôÙcal ediôÙtion of CapôÙiôÙtal VolôÙume 1, and itãs the text used in the course. If youãre interôÙestôÙed in delvôÙing deepôÙer into Marxãs CapôÙiôÙtal, see the David HarôÙvey coursôÙes listôÙed in the RelatôÙeds below.
This course will be added to our list of 1,700 Free Online CoursôÙes from Top UniôÙverôÙsiôÙties

title: Discover the First Horror & Fantasy Magazine, Der Orchideengarten, and Its Bizarre Artwork (1919ã1921)
description: From the 18th cenôÙtuôÙry onward, the genôÙres of GothôÙic horôÙror and fanôÙtaôÙsy have flourôÙished, and with them the senôÙsuôÙalôÙly visôÙcerôÙal images n
body:
From the 18th cenôÙtuôÙry onward, the genôÙres of GothôÙic horôÙror and fanôÙtaôÙsy have flourôÙished, and with them the senôÙsuôÙalôÙly visôÙcerôÙal images now comôÙmonôÙplace in film, TV, and comôÙic books. These genôÙres perôÙhaps reached their aesôÙthetôÙic peak in the 19th cenôÙtuôÙry with writôÙers like Edgar Allan Poe and illusôÙtraôÙtors like GusôÙtave Dore. But it was in the earôÙly twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry that a more popôÙulist subôÙgenre truôÙly came into its own: ãweird ficôÙtion,ã a term H.P. LoveôÙcraft used to describe the pulpy brand of superôÙnatôÙurôÙal horôÙror codôÙiôÙfied in the pages of AmerôÙiôÙcan fanôÙtaôÙsy and horôÙror magôÙaôÙzine Weird Talesãfirst pubôÙlished in 1923. (And still going strong!)

A preôÙcurôÙsor to EC Comicsã many lurid titles, Weird Tales is often conôÙsidôÙered the definôÙiôÙtive earôÙly twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry venue for weird ficôÙtion and illusôÙtraôÙtion.
But we need only look back a few years and to anothôÙer conôÙtiôÙnent to find an earôÙliôÙer pubôÙliôÙcaôÙtion, servôÙing GerôÙman-speakôÙing fansãDer OrchideenôÙgarten (ãThe GarôÙden of Orchidsã), the very first horôÙror and fanôÙtaôÙsy magôÙaôÙzine, which ran 51 issues from JanôÙuôÙary 1919 to NovemôÙber 1921.

The magôÙaôÙzine feaôÙtured work from its ediôÙtors Karl Hans Strobl and Alfons von CzibulôÙka, from betôÙter-known conôÙtemôÙpoôÙraries like H.G. Wells and Karel Capek, and from foreôÙfaôÙthers like DickôÙens, Pushkin, Guy de MauôÙpasôÙsant, Poe, Voltaire, WashôÙingôÙton IrvôÙing, Nathaniel Hawthorne, and othôÙers. ãAlthough two issues of Der OrchideenôÙgarten were devotôÙed to detecôÙtive stoôÙries,ã writes 50 Watts, ãand one to erotôÙic stoôÙries about cuckôÙolds, it was a genôÙuine fanôÙtaôÙsy magôÙaôÙzine.ã And it was also a gallery of bizarre and unusuôÙal artôÙwork.

50 Watts quotes from Franz Rottensteinerãs descripôÙtion of the magazineãs art, which rangedô ãfrom repôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtions of medieval woodôÙcuts to the work of masôÙters of the macabre such as GusôÙtave Dore or Tony JohanôÙnot, to conôÙtemôÙpoôÙrary GerôÙman artists like Rolf von HoerôÙschelôÙmann, Otto LennekoôÙgel, Karl RitôÙter, HeinôÙrich Kley, or Alfred Kubin.ã These artists creôÙatôÙed the covôÙers and illusôÙtraôÙtions you see here, and many more you can see atô 50 Watts, the black sun, and John Coulthartãs {feuilôÙleton}.

ãWhat strikes me about these black-and-white drawôÙings,ã like the dense, frenôÙzied pen-and-ink scene above, Coulthart comôÙments, ãis how difôÙferôÙent they are in tone to the pulp magôÙaôÙzines which folôÙlowed shortôÙly after in AmerôÙiôÙca and elseôÙwhere. Theyãre at once far more adult and freôÙquentôÙly more origôÙiôÙnal than the GothôÙic clichûˋs which padded out Weird Tales and lessôÙer titles for many years.ã Indeed, though the forôÙmat may be simôÙiôÙlar to its sucôÙcesôÙsors, Der OrchideenôÙgartenãs covôÙers show the influôÙence of SurôÙreôÙalôÙism, ãsome are almost ExpresôÙsionôÙist in style,ã and many of the illusôÙtraôÙtions show ãa disôÙtinct Goya influôÙence.ã

PopôÙuôÙlar fanôÙtaôÙsy and horôÙror illusôÙtraôÙtion has often leaned more toward the soft-porn of sevôÙenôÙties airôÙbrushed vans, pulp-novôÙel covôÙers, or the grisôÙlyô kitsch of the comics. RotôÙtenôÙsteinôÙer writes in his 1978 FanôÙtaôÙsy Book that this ãlarge-forôÙmat magôÙaôÙzineãÎ must sureôÙly rank as one of the most beauôÙtiôÙful fanôÙtaôÙsy magôÙaôÙzines ever pubôÙlished.ã Itãs hard to argue with that assessôÙment. View, read (in GerôÙman), and downôÙload origôÙiôÙnal scans of the magazineãs first sevôÙerôÙal issues over on this PrinceôÙton site.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
If you would like to sign up for Open Cultureãs free email newsletôÙter,ô please find it here. Itãs a great way to see our new posts, all bunôÙdled in one email, each day.
If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
How Georges Mûˋliû´sô A Trip to the Moonô Became the First Sci-Fi Film & Changed CinôÙeôÙma ForôÙevôÙer (1902)
Read MarôÙgaret Cavendishãsô The BlazôÙing World: The First Sci-Fi NovôÙel WritôÙten By a Woman (1666)
The First Work of SciôÙence FicôÙtion: Read Lucianãs 2nd-CenôÙtuôÙry Space TravôÙelôÙogueô A True StoôÙry
Free: 356 Issues ofô Galaxy, the GroundôÙbreakôÙing 1950s SciôÙence FicôÙtion MagôÙaôÙzine
Josh Jonesô is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC. FolôÙlow him atô @jdmagness

title: Stitch Your Favorite Destinations with Jake Henzler's 'Knit the City'
description:
All images courtesy of Jake Henzler and David & Charles Publishing, shared with permission
April 1, 2026


When we visit major hubs like Copenhagen o
body:
All images courtesy of Jake Henzler and David & Charles Publishing, shared with permission
April 1, 2026


When we visit major hubs like Copenhagen or Paris, we often take a lot of photos and make sure to grab a little souvenir as a memento of our visit. How better to remember the architecture and the feel of the city? Well, fiber designer Jake Henzler, a.k.a. Boy Knits World, figures you can stitch those memories into something much cozier than a postcard or a keychain.
Forthcoming from David & Charles Publishing, Henzlerãs book Knit the City highlights buildings around the world through a series of building block-like patterns. Using a modular system, details like gables and windows can be switched up to create your own unique facades. Then itãs up to you to choose the colors youãd like to use. The blocks can then be stitched together to create a blanket, pillow covers, or any other application you can come up with.
Knit the City is slated for release on May 5. Pre-order your copy in the Colossal Shop. You can also find Henzlerãs patterns on Ravelry.

schema.headline: Stitch Your Favorite Destinations with Jake Henzler's 'Knit the City'

title: Barry Webb Documents a Marvelous, Macro Array of Colorful Slime Molds
description: Blown wildly out of proportion in large format, the slime molds that British photographer Barry Webb captures seem atmospheric and sculptural. Ste
body:
Blown wildly out of proportion in large format, the slime molds that British photographer Barry Webb captures seem atmospheric and sculptural. Stemonitis, for example, looks like dozens of thin pieces of wire with their ends coated in colored wax. But this fungi-like form is one of hundreds of kinds of slime mold, and it typically only reaches a height of about two centimeters at the most. Thanks to Webbãs macro photos, we glimpse a phenomenally beautiful world up-close that is otherwise virtually invisible.
Scientists have documented hundreds of these organisms, which arenãt actually related to plants, fungi, animals, or moldsãdespite the name. They comprise a unique group unto themselves, more closely related to amoebas. And new discoveries are being made all the time. From mottled gray bulbs that look like snow-covered trees to pink, coral-like tendrils, Webb chronicles a huge array of colors and shapes. He also consistently submits images to local and national botanical records so that researchers have access to high-resolution imagery.
Didymium squamulosum
Webbãs image of a species called Lamproderma scintillans, partly engulfed by a water droplet, won the Botanical Britain category of the British Wildlife Photography Awards. Several of his photos are on display in large format in the exhibition Mythos Wald at Gasometer Oberhausen in Germany, which continues through the end of the year. And in the U.K., see Webbãs awarded images in the 2026 International Garden Photographer of the Year exhibition at Cambridge University Botanic Garden. Find more on his Instagram.
Cribraria argillacea
Cribraria aurantiaca
Physarum psittacinum and tiny mites
Lamproderma on top of Trichia flavicoma
Deformed Stemonitis
Pink Arcyria

title: Semiprecious Stones Coat Kathleen Ryan's Oversized Sculptures of Rotting Food
description: There are thousands upon thousands of types of mold out there. Some you can eatãthink the rind on a wheel of brie or a gray fungus known as ãnoble
body:
There are thousands upon thousands of types of mold out there. Some you can eatãthink the rind on a wheel of brie or a gray fungus known as ãnoble rotã that gives certain types of grapes an extra sweet flavor for dessert wines. But there are plenty we shouldnãt eat, and when that loaf of bread in the cupboard begins to turn blue-green, itãs definitely time to chuck it in the bin. For Kathleen Ryan, the myriad colors and textures of mold continue to inspire larger-than-life sculptures of fruit and other foods that, in a way, preserve decay.
Ryanãs oversized works are characterized by their textural finishes, often using salvaged metal and other materials in addition to an array of colored beads and semiprecious stones to achieve the effects of layered fungi and rot. Recent works such as ãBad Lemon (Slice of Paradise)ã and ãScrewdriverã nod to the realm of cocktails and, by extension, the notion of luxury and even vacationsãconcepts that somewhat sour within the context of an increasingly vulnerable economy.
ãBad Lemon (Slice of Paradise)ã (2024), serpentine, prehnite, azurite in malachite, amazonite, aquamarine, jasper, dolomite, and fuchsite, pyrite, turquoise, labradorite, agate, marble, steel pins on coated polystyrene, Volkswagen fender, 14 x 26 1/2 x 11 1/2 inches
Juxtaposing stones ranging from amethyst and azurite to turquoise and tourmaline with salvaged metal from vintage cars, Ryanãs sculptures evoke an array of associations. She has previously likened their over-the-top scale to the roadside attractions tourists might see along Interstate highways, such as giant doughnuts and other foods and animals.
Like a geode that doesnãt look like much from the outside, works like ãBad Lemon (Slice of Paradise)ã have two very different personalities, where the metal exterior lets on little about whatãs inside. Ryan taps into our appreciation of hidden beauty when opening up an ancient, crystallized stone while simultaneously suggesting the grotesqueness of opening a peach, for example, only to find it rotten inside.
Some of the works seen here were recently on view at Karma in New York, and you can find more on Ryanãs Instagram.
Detail of ãBad Lemon (Slice of Paradise)ã
ãScrewdriverã (2023), onyx, citrine, rhodonite, garnet, agate, tektite, lava rock, turquoise, aquamarine, serpentine, magnesite, amazonite, black tourmaline, jasper, prehnite, ruby in zoisite, marble, amber, labradorite, smoky quartz, quartz, acrylic, steel pins on coated polystyrene, aluminum umbrella, 68ã AMC Javelin trunk, 77 x 88 x 107 inches
Detail of ãScrewdriverã
ãBad Lemon (Desert)ã (2023), citrine, jasper, agate, smoky quartz, quartz, carnelian, calcite, labradorite, amber, sunstone, garnet, unakite, red aventurine, tigerãs eye, tourmaline, hessonite garnet, chrysoprase, lodolite, lepidolite, serpentine, shell, freshwater pearl, glass, steel pins on coated polystyrene, 16 1/2 x 17 x 14 inches
ãBad Lemon (Desert)ã
Detail of ãBad Lemon (Desert)ã
Detail of ãSunset Stripã
Detail of ãSunset Stripã

title: Anoushka Mirchandani Conjures Ancient Mythological Nature Spirits in Vibrant Oil Paintings
description: Throughout Southeast Asia, nymph-like, shape-shifting deities associated with clouds and water known as apsaras are commonly depicted in sculpture
body:
Throughout Southeast Asia, nymph-like, shape-shifting deities associated with clouds and water known as apsaras are commonly depicted in sculptures and other artworks dating back millennia. For San Francisco-based artist Anoushka Mirchandani, who was born in India, these mythological beings are the spirits, so to speak, of vibrant oil paintings.
Tapping into family memories and her upbringing influenced by South Asian cultural traditions, Mirchandani explores mythology and perception. Her current solo exhibition, My Body Was a River Once at ICA San Josûˋ, explores the tradition of the apsara through a lens of timelessness, femininity, and biophilia. Curated by Zoû¨ Latner, the show emphasizes the dynamic between power and vulnerability, exemplified by Mirchandaniãs approach to the figuresã presence and ease amid the unpredictability of nature.
ãBy the Perequûˆ-ûûÏuã (2025), oil and oil pastel on canvas, 48 x 36 inches
As a child, Mirchandani observed apsaras in the ancient Buddhist caves of Ajanta and Ellora. ãThese water-women are beings of transformation, embodying sensuality, cosmic energy, and the transmission of matrilineal knowledge,ã says an exhibition statement.
In the paintings, figures are at one with their surroundings, virtually faceless with the exception of shadows that suggest lips and noses. The outlines of their bodies merge with tropical plants, moss, boulders, and flowing streams, and their flesh is partly transparent. Additionally, the underpainting of each work comprises a terracotta red, inspired by the clay-rich soil of the Indian states of Goa and Maharashtra. Starting with this earthy pigment literally grounds Mirchandaniãs compositions in a sense of home and belonging.
My Body Was a River Once continues through August 23. See more on Mirchandaniãs Instagram.
ãRites of Returnã (2025), oil, oil pastel, and oil stick on canvas, 80 x 73 inches
Left panel of ãAll Us Come Cross The Waterã
Right panel of ãAll Us Come Cross The Waterã
ãCherry Springsã (2025), oil, oil pastel, and oil stick on canvas, 40 x 30 inches
ãYou Could Pull The Tide In By Her Hairã (2025), oil, oil pastel, and oil stick on canvas, 60 x 48 inches
ãVanishing Pointã (2025), oil, oil pastel, and oil stick on canvas, 40 x 30 inches
ãTo Tear a Hundred Veilsã (2025), oil, oil pastel, and oil stick on canvas, 60 x 45 inches
